text: The surgeon begins the procedure with a wide Cocker maneuver, suspending the duodenum from four corners. A laparoscopic ultrasound is used to localize the ampulla, followed by a longitudinal duodenotomy. Two stitches are placed superiorly and inferiorly to the ampulla for mobilization and retraction during dissection. Circumferential dissection is carried down to the pancreas using monopolar scissors. The biliary and pancreatic ducts are identified and transected without energy. The common bile duct is spatulated with POTS scissors, and a choledochoduodenostomy is performed using interrupted V-O vial stitches.

The surgeon secures the common bile duct to the pancreatic duct, forming a new septum. A running IVO bicryl stitch is used to take full-thickness bites of the pancreas and duodenum, repairing the duodenal defect. The stitch is continued inferiorly to incorporate the duodenal mucosa. A second running stitch is initiated on the contralateral side.

The surgeon runs the stitch inferiorly to meet the contralateral stitch, tying them together to complete the reconstruction of the posterior wall of the duodenum. The duodenal anatomy is closed in a transverse fashion with a preferred two-layer closure, starting with a 4-0 running bicryl suture.

The surgeon closes the second layer of the dual anatomy with a 3-0 bicurl Connell stitch, completing the closure. The suture line is inspected, and a drain is placed along the dual anatomy. The final appearance shows the closed dual anatomy without immediate complications.
